original_abstract:
Abstract

The biochemical profiling of *Rosa canina* L. (dog rose) is a subject of significant pharmacological interest due to the high levels of ascorbic acid, phenolics, and flavonoids, which contribute to its potent antioxidant, antimutagenic, and anticarcinogenic properties. This study evaluates the phytochemical composition—specifically vitamin C, total polyphenols, and total flavonoids—and the corresponding antioxidant activities of eight *Rosa canina* biotypes sourced from the spontaneous flora of Transylvania, Romania.

Chromatographic and spectrophotometric analyses revealed substantial variability among the studied genotypes. Ascorbic acid content ranged from 112.20 mg/100 g in low-altitude samples (var. *assiensis*) to 360.22 mg/100 g in high-altitude samples (var. *transitoria* f. *ramosissima*), demonstrating a strong positive correlation between vitamin C accumulation and elevation. Total polyphenol content varied between 326.3 and 575.1 mg GAE/100 g, while total flavonoids ranged from 101.3 to 163.2 mg QE/100 g. Antioxidant capacity, determined via the DPPH free radical scavenging assay, showed a notable correlation with both ascorbic acid and total polyphenol concentrations, though it was negatively correlated with flavonoid content.

Two specific biotypes from the Bistrita-Nasaud region, var. *transitoria* f. *ramosissima* and var. *transitoria* f. *montivaga*, were identified as superior sources of bioactive compounds. These results suggest that these high-altitude Transylvanian genotypes possess significant potential for use in the functional food and nutraceutical industries as natural antioxidant sources and could serve as valuable genetic material for future crop improvement programs.
humanized_abstract:
Abstract

Rosa canina L. (dog rose) biochemical analysis has been of high pharmacological interest, due to the presence of ascorbic acid, phenolics and flavonoids, with confirmed antioxidant, antimutagenic and anticancer activities. The aim of this study was to investigate the vitamin C content, total polyphenols and total flavonoids as well as antioxidant activity of 8 *Rosa canina* biotypes collected from the spontaneous flora of Transylvania, Romania.

Various chromatic and spectrophotometric analyses have shown a high degree of variation among the plant materials genotypes. The ascorbic acid content ranged from 112.20 mg/100g (in *assiensis* variety and low altitude) to 360.22 mg/100g (in *transitoria* f. *ramosissima* variety and high altitude), in all cases with a highly significant direct correlation. The total polyphenols varied between 326.3-575.1 mg GAE/100g and the total flavonoids between 101.3-163.2 mg QE/100g. The antioxidant capacity by DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) free radical scavenging assay showed a significant correlation with the content of ascorbic acid and total polyphenols and also a negative correlation with the flavonoids content.

In this study, two important sheperd's cress biotypes from the Bistrita-Nasaud region, var. * transitoria* f. *ramosissima* and var. * transitoria* f. *montivaga*, were demonstrated to have the highest content of bioactive compounds and consequently are highly recommended for future evaluation. The Altitudinal Transylvanian genotypes thus have large industrial application as natural antioxidants for functional food and nutraceutical products and can also serve as valuable materials in plant breeding programs.
